did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically a stacking ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including DenseNet121, ResNet50, EfficientNetB2, MobileNet, SE-InceptionV3, and VGG16, for multi-class classification of skin cancer dermoscopy images. It also integrated meta-data and applied cost-sensitive learning for risk management.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a stacking ensemble deep learning model for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodologies including data preprocessing, CNN architectures (DenseNet121, ResNet50, etc.), meta-data integration, and cost-sensitive learning. It reports original results, such as a maximum accuracy of 91% on a validation dataset, and describes the experimental setup using datasets like BCN_20000 and HAM10000. Although published in a conference proceedings (2020 International Computer Symposium), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Cancer Dermoscopy Images Classification with Meta Data via Deep Learning Ensemble
brief_summary: This study proposes a stacking ensemble deep learning model for multi-class classification of skin cancer dermoscopy images, integrating image preprocessing, meta-data, and risk management. The authors utilized six pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, ResNet50, EfficientNetB2, MobileNet, SE-InceptionV3, and VGG16) trained on the BCN_20000 and HAM10000 datasets, which include eight diagnostic categories. Meta-data such as age, sex, and anatomical site were concatenated with CNN probability matrices to form a second-level ensemble classifier. Evaluation on a 5% holdout validation dataset demonstrated that the ensemble model achieved a maximum accuracy of 91%, outperforming the best single CNN, DenseNet121, which reached 87.3%. Additionally, the study introduced a cost-sensitive learning approach in the ensemble phase to manage risk trade-offs, such as maximizing melanoma recall, without requiring retraining of the base CNNs.

all_task_types.target: Eight diagnostic categories of skin cancer (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion, Squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of skin cancer dermoscopy images using a stacking ensemble of CNNs and meta-data. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (max 91%), precision, and recall rate. Cost-sensitive learning was applied to manipulate trade-offs between precision and recall for specific categories like Melanoma, but the output remains class prediction.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To manipulate risk management trade-offs between precision and recall rate for specific categories (e.g., Melanoma) by assigning penalty costs to false items, thereby training the classifier to minimize total cost rather than maximizing accuracy.
To evaluate the performance of the ensemble model using a 5% holdout validation dataset.
To breakdown performance details, including accuracy, precision, and recall rate for each class and model configuration.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, and recall rate on a holdout validation dataset, with comparisons made based on absolute metric values rather than statistical significance.

BCN_20000, HAM10000, MSKDataset (Combine.description: Total 25,331 images split into 95% training and 5% validation holdout set.

architecture_used.main_model: Stacking Ensemble of six pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (DenseNet121, ResNet50, EfficientNetB2, MobileNet, SE-InceptionV3, VGG16) combined with meta-data, classified by a second-level meta-classifier (RandomForest, SVM, JRip, etc.).
architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet pre-trained weights for all six base CNN models.
architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels for DenseNet121, ResNet50, MobileNet, SE-InceptionV3, and VGG16; 260x260 pixels for EfficientNetB2.

architecture_details.base_model_configuration: Models used without top layers (feature extraction), followed by new fully connected layers.
architecture_details.ensemble_structure: Probability matrices from base CNNs concatenated with meta-data, then fed into a meta-classifier.

training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported (used Keras predict() and standard training flows).
training_details.loss_function: Class weighted mode used to prevent loss bias from imbalanced samples; Cost-sensitive learning applied in the ensemble phase via cost matrix adjustment rather than CNN loss function.
training_details.epochs: 5 epochs of transfer learning followed by 15-20 epochs of fine-tuning for mature CNNs.
training_details.hardware: NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti graphics cards.

classification_or_output_head.output_method: Probability matrix from Keras’ predict() per CNN, concatenated with meta-data, then processed by meta-classifier.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing (to 224x224 pixels for DenseNet121, ResNet50, MobileNet, SE-InceptionV3, VGG16; to 260x260 pixels for EfficientNetB2)
Pixel data normalization using Keras’ preprocess_input for each model

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, flip, shear, and 'room' (likely a typo for zoom or random crop) were applied during training. These transforms increased the total number of images per category by up to 6 times per epoch.
White balance adjustment was applied as a preprocessing/augmentation step to improve accuracy by 1% to 2% compared to datasets without it.
TTA was evaluated but showed no contribution to performance in this study.

conclusions: The proposed stacking ensemble deep learning model integrating CNNs and meta-data achieved a maximum accuracy of 91% on a 5% holdout validation dataset, outperforming the best single CNN (DenseNet121) which reached 87.3%.

The ensemble approach provided significant performance backup (approximately 6% accuracy improvement) for premature or weak CNNs, whereas mature CNNs showed no significant contribution from the ensemble approach alone.
Meta-data (age, sex, anatomical site) contributed to accuracy improvements of 1% to 2% when integrated with single CNNs that were in a premature or weaker status.
White balance adjustment as a preprocessing step contributed to a 1% to 2% accuracy improvement compared to datasets without it.
Test Time Augmentation (TTA) showed no contribution to performance in this study.
A second-level ensemble of meta-classifiers (stacking, bagging, AdaBoost) showed no additional contribution to performance compared to using individual meta-classifiers.
RandomForest, SVM, and JRip performed well as meta-classifiers for the concatenated data of CNN probability matrices and meta-data.

Cost-sensitive learning applied in the ensemble phase allowed for manipulation of risk management trade-offs, such as maximizing Melanoma recall rate, by adjusting the cost matrix without requiring retraining of the base CNNs via loss function adjustment.
The cost-sensitive approach achieved a very good recall rate for Melanoma but resulted in sacrifices in overall accuracy, precision, and recall rates for other categories.
important_remarks: The study relies on a 5% holdout validation set for performance evaluation, lacking an independent external test set or cross-validation, which limits the assessment of generalizability.
The dataset (25,331 images) combines BCN_20000, HAM10000, and MSKDataset, but the paper does not report per-class sample distributions, raising concerns about class imbalance and potential bias.
Meta-data (age, sex, anatomical site) contained missing values that were imputed using averages or new nominal values, which may introduce noise or bias into the model.

The cost-sensitive learning approach for Melanoma recall optimization was applied in the ensemble phase via cost matrix adjustment rather than during CNN training, avoiding retraining but potentially limiting the depth of feature adaptation for high-risk classes.

Test Time Augmentation (TTA) and second-level ensemble methods (bagging, AdaBoost) showed no performance contribution, suggesting the proposed stacking ensemble with meta-data is sufficient for this specific setup.
The model was trained on NVIDIA GeForce GTX 1080 Ti hardware, which may limit reproducibility for researchers without similar GPU resources.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the reported accuracy improvements, making it difficult to assess the robustness of the observed gains.

Clinical relevance is limited by the lack of discussion on deployment constraints, such as inference speed or integration with clinical workflows, despite the use of lightweight models like MobileNet.
